title: Trees
description: Tree problems are interview favorites. Master the TreeNode class, DFS traversals (preorder, inorder, postorder), BFS level-order traversal, and depth computation in JavaScript.

sections.title: TreeNode class
sections.explanation: Define a simple TreeNode with `val`, `left`, and `right`. In interviews, you'll usually be given this class, but know how to write it from scratch. Default left and right to null. Build trees by linking nodes manually for testing.

exercise.prompt: Define a TreeNode class. Build a tree:  1 -> left: 2, right: 3. Node 2 -> left: 4, right: 5. Print root.val, root.left.val, root.right.val, root.left.left.val, root.left.right.val.

validation.message: Tree structure: root=1, left=2, right=3, left.left=4, left.right=5
Define TreeNode with val, left, and right properties

exercise.hint: root.left = new TreeNode(2), root.right = new TreeNode(3).

sections.title: DFS traversals (preorder, inorder, postorder)
sections.explanation: DFS traversals visit nodes in different orders: preorder (root, left, right) for copying trees, inorder (left, root, right) for BST sorted order, postorder (left, right, root) for deletion and bottom-up computation. Know all three — interviewers pick whichever fits the problem.

exercise.prompt: Build tree: 4 -> left: 2, right: 6. Node 2 -> left: 1, right: 3. Node 6 -> left: 5, right: 7. Implement inorder DFS and print the result joined by commas.

validation.message: Inorder traversal of this BST gives sorted order: 1,2,3,4,5,6,7
Recurse on left and right subtrees with the result array

exercise.hint: Base case: node === null. Push node.val between left and right recursive calls.

sections.title: BFS level-order traversal
sections.explanation: BFS processes nodes level by level using a queue. For level-order grouping, track the queue size at each level start. This pattern solves level averages, zigzag traversal, and rightmost-node problems. Use shift() for dequeue.

exercise.prompt: Build tree: 1 -> left: 2, right: 3. Node 2 -> left: 4, right: 5. Implement BFS level-order and print each level as comma-joined values on its own line.

validation.message: Use shift for dequeue and push to add children to the queue

exercise.hint: shift() dequeues from front. Push node.left and node.right into the queue.

sections.title: Tree depth
sections.explanation: Max depth is the length of the longest path from root to a leaf. This is a classic recursive problem: depth = 1 + max(depth(left), depth(right)). Base case: null node has depth 0. This pattern extends to balanced tree checks and diameter computation.

exercise.prompt: Build tree: 1 -> left: 2 -> left: 4 -> left: 8. Right of 1: 3. Compute and print the max depth.

validation.message: Use Math.max with recursive calls on left and right subtrees

exercise.hint: Base case returns 0. Use Math.max to take the deeper subtree.
